title: Uniform Sets with Few Progressions via Colorings
speaker: Yufei Zhao
abstract: Ruzsa asked whether there exist Fourier-uniform subsets of ℤ/Nℤ with very few 4-term arithmetic progressions (4-AP). The standard pedagogical example of a Fourier uniform set with a "wrong" density of 4-APs actually has 4-AP density much higher than random. Can it instead be much lower than random? Gowers constructed Fourier uniform sets with 4-AP density at most α4+c. It remains open whether a superpolynomial decay is possible. We will discuss this question and some variants. We relate it to an arithmetic Ramsey question: can one No(1)-color of [N] avoiding symmetrically-colored 4-APs?

proof_stages.label: Fourier-uniformity and the 4AP lower-tail problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from Roth's theorem: Fourier-uniform sets have random-like 3AP counts, but Fourier-uniformity does not force random-like 4AP counts, so the relevant question is whether Fourier-uniform sets can have substantially fewer than random 4APs.

proof_stages.label: Ruzsa/Gowers rho_4 formulation and known bounds
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formalizes the extremal lower density rho_4(alpha), states the conjectural faster-than-polynomial decay in alpha, and reviews the history and current quantitative upper bounds.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric-coloring conjecture and finite-coloring bridge
proof_stages.summary: A Ramsey-type conjecture is introduced: color [n] with n^{o(1)} colors while avoiding 4APs with color pattern abba, and any finite coloring with this property gives an upper bound on rho_4 through a tensor-power argument.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic torus model and rho-tilde
proof_stages.summary: The talk restricts to a broad quadratic-Fourier construction f_n(x)=F(x/n,x^2/n) with constant first marginal, identifies the limiting 4AP functional Lambda-tilde_4(F), defines rho-tilde_4, and explains both its equivalence to the coloring conjecture and the limitations of proving this model class fully universal.

proof_stages.label: Coloring-to-model construction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming an r-coloring with no symmetrically colored 4APs, the proof constructs a torus function F using a set with no nontrivial solutions to a-3b+3c-d=0, obtaining constant first marginal and small Lambda-tilde_4.

proof_stages.label: Model-to-coloring extraction
proof_stages.summary: Conversely, from a model function F with small Lambda-tilde_4, the proof samples a random horizontal arithmetic progression and random vertical coordinates to define a coloring, then uses a Cauchy-Schwarz-type calculation to rule out symmetrically colored 4APs.

proof_stages.label: Longer and nonuniform progression extensions
proof_stages.summary: The framework is extended to k-term APs and specified-spacing patterns: odd lengths or nonsymmetric spacings are tractable, while even symmetric patterns reduce to corresponding symmetric-coloring problems, with several open variants raised in questions.

examples.name: Quadratic phase interval set
examples.note: The set of residues whose square modulo n lies in an interval is Fourier-uniform but has many 4APs, prompting the shift from random-like 4AP counts to the lower-tail question of whether Fourier-uniform sets can have too few 4APs.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the quadratic-phase interval set, invoking calculations for Fourier uniformity and using an algebraic identity to explain why its 4AP density is about the 3AP density, roughly alpha^2 rather than alpha^4.

passes.reason: the quadratic-phase interval set is the standard example showing Fourier uniformity need not give random 4AP counts.

examples.name: Random coloring with the Lovász local lemma
examples.note: Random coloring is offered as a benchmark showing that about n^{1/2} colors suffice by the local lemma, illustrating how much stronger the n^{o(1)} coloring conjecture would be.
examples.engagement_reason: Random coloring with the Lovász local lemma is cited as a benchmark giving about sqrt(n) colors, but the local-lemma argument is not carried out.

examples.name: Behrend sphere-level-set construction
examples.note: Behrend-type large 4AP-free sets are cited as the familiar way to make large progression-free color classes, but they are bad at avoiding symmetrically colored 4APs, emphasizing the difficulty of the coloring conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: The Behrend sphere-level-set construction is described at a high level as producing large 4AP-free sets and many symmetrically colored 4APs, without deriving or running the construction.

passes.reason: Behrend’s sphere-level-set construction is highly symmetric through its spherical level sets and is cited as a known construction with limitations.
